Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]][[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg |location = [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Echizen, Fukui|Echizen]], Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.900888888888886|N|136.12658333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |consecration_year = 7th year of Emperor Sujin |MainHallStyle = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] |Festival = October 10 |Rituals = Onigadake Fire Festival |Map = {{location map|Japan Fukui Prefecture|label=Ōmushi Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} |location = {{ill|Echizen|WD=Q877941}} }} '''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Ōmushi Town, [[:en:Echizen, Fukui|Echizen]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) and a former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine is dedicated to the deity {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was established in the 7th year of Emperor Sujin’s reign, when the spirit of Hoori, who subdued and developed the Nan'etsu region, was enshrined at the summit of Mt. Onigadake. During Emperor Suinin's reign, a locust infestation was averted after prayers at the shrine, leading the emperor to name it Ōmushi Shrine and move it to its current location. The first historical mention appears in the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], which records that Ōmushi Shrine of Echizen Province's Nyū District was granted the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, in the ppth year of Hōki (780). By the 3rd year of Tentoku (959), it had risen to the highest Rank, Senior First Rank. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} as one of only two {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in Echizen Province, it held great importance. In 1576, the shrine buildings were burned down by Shibata Katsuie, but the sacred treasures were safely relocated to the summit of Mt. Onigadake. They were later restored under the order of Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1583, the shrine incorporated smaller shrines, including {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}, Amayo Shrine, and Raijin Shrine, although the latter two have contested identification. The shrine’s priesthood has traditionally been held by the Okano family. Historical records confirm the family’s service from at least the Nanboku-chō period. Despite losing some members during Shibata Katsuie’s conflicts, the family lineage continues unbroken. The current chief priest, Okano Yoshinori, is considered the 100th in line. During the Edo period, some members of the family served as samurai in Akō Domain, with connections to the famous [[:en:Forty-seven rōnin|Forty-seven rōnin]]. == Facilities == Two seated wooden male deity statues from the Heian period, said to represent {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}{{CulturalHeritageOnline|154691|木造男神坐像（伝天津日高日子穂穂出見命）}} and Shiotsuchi-no-Oji{{CulturalHeritageOnline|202150|木造男神坐像（伝塩椎神）}}, are designated as Important Cultural Properties of Japan. A large rock enshrined beside the main building, known as Ōiwa-sama, serves as a sacred object in Ōiwa Shrine. The shrine also has an inner shrine at the summit of Mt. Onigadake and a spring called "Ishigami no Yusui," which supplied the local water system until 1994. File:Oiwa shrine.jpg|Ōiwa Shrine File:Oiwa shrine object of worship (Oiwa-sama).jpg|Sacred object of Ōiwa Shrine (Ōiwa-sama) File:Ishigami's spring water.jpg|Ishigami no Yusui == Festivals == On August 15, the "Onigadake Fire Festival," in which participants ascend Mt. Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{translated page|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg |location = [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Echizen, Fukui|Echizen]], Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.900888888888886|N|136.12658333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})

Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]][[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg |location = [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Echizen, Fukui|Echizen]], Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.900888888888886|N|136.12658333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |consecration_year = 7th year of Emperor Sujin |MainHallStyle = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] |Festival = October 10 |Rituals = Onigadake Fire Festival |Map = {{location map|Japan Fukui Prefecture|label=Ōmushi Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} |location = {{ill|Echizen|WD=Q877941}} }} '''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Ōmushi Town, [[:en:Echizen, Fukui|Echizen]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) and a former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine is dedicated to the deity {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was established in the 7th year of Emperor Sujin’s reign, when the spirit of Hoori, who subdued and developed the Nan'etsu region, was enshrined at the summit of Mt. Onigadake. During Emperor Suinin's reign, a locust infestation was averted after prayers at the shrine, leading the emperor to name it Ōmushi Shrine and move it to its current location. The first historical mention appears in the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], which records that Ōmushi Shrine of Echizen Province's Nyū District was granted the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, in the ppth year of Hōki (780). By the 3rd year of Tentoku (959), it had risen to the highest Rank, Senior First Rank. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} as one of only two {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in Echizen Province, it held great importance. In 1576, the shrine buildings were burned down by Shibata Katsuie, but the sacred treasures were safely relocated to the summit of Mt. Onigadake. They were later restored under the order of Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1583, the shrine incorporated smaller shrines, including {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}, Amayo Shrine, and Raijin Shrine, although the latter two have contested identification. The shrine’s priesthood has traditionally been held by the Okano family. Historical records confirm the family’s service from at least the Nanboku-chō period. Despite losing some members during Shibata Katsuie’s conflicts, the family lineage continues unbroken. The current chief priest, Okano Yoshinori, is considered the 100th in line. During the Edo period, some members of the family served as samurai in Akō Domain, with connections to the famous [[:en:Forty-seven rōnin|Forty-seven rōnin]]. == Facilities == Two seated wooden male deity statues from the Heian period, said to represent {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}{{CulturalHeritageOnline|154691|木造男神坐像（伝天津日高日子穂穂出見命）}} and Shiotsuchi-no-Oji{{CulturalHeritageOnline|202150|木造男神坐像（伝塩椎神）}}, are designated as Important Cultural Properties of Japan. A large rock enshrined beside the main building, known as Ōiwa-sama, serves as a sacred object in Ōiwa Shrine. The shrine also has an inner shrine at the summit of Mt. Onigadake and a spring called "Ishigami no Yusui," which supplied the local water system until 1994. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. File:Oiwa shrine.jpg|Ōiwa Shrine File:Oiwa shrine object of worship (Oiwa-sama).jpg|Sacred object of Ōiwa Shrine (Ōiwa-sama) File:Ishigami's spring water.jpg|Ishigami no Yusui == Festivals == On August 15, the "Onigadake Fire Festival," in which participants ascend Mt. Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]][[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg |location = [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Echizen, Fukui|Echizen]], Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.900888888888886|N|136.12658333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |consecration_year = 7th year of Emperor Sujin |MainHallStyle = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] |Festival = October 10 |Rituals = Onigadake Fire Festival |Map = {{location map|Japan Fukui Prefecture|label=Ōmushi Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} |location = {{ill|Echizen|WD=Q877941}} }} '''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Ōmushi Town, [[:en:Echizen, Fukui|Echizen]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]]. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) and a former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine is dedicated to the deity {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was established in the 7th year of Emperor Sujin’s reign, when the spirit of Hoori, who subdued and developed the Nan'etsu region, was enshrined at the summit of Mt. Onigadake. During Emperor Suinin's reign, a locust infestation was averted after prayers at the shrine, leading the emperor to name it Ōmushi Shrine and move it to its current location. The first historical mention appears in the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], which records that Ōmushi Shrine of Echizen Province's Nyū District was granted the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, in the ppth year of Hōki (780). By the 3rd year of Tentoku (959), it had risen to the highest Rank, Senior First Rank. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} as one of only two {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in Echizen Province, it held great importance. In 1576, the shrine buildings were burned down by Shibata Katsuie, but the sacred treasures were safely relocated to the summit of Mt. Onigadake. They were later restored under the order of Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1583, the shrine incorporated smaller shrines, including {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}, Amayo Shrine, and Raijin Shrine, although the latter two have contested identification. The shrine’s priesthood has traditionally been held by the Okano family. Historical records confirm the family’s service from at least the Nanboku-chō period. Despite losing some members during Shibata Katsuie’s conflicts, the family lineage continues unbroken. The current chief priest, Okano Yoshinori, is considered the 100th in line. During the Edo period, some members of the family served as samurai in Akō Domain, with connections to the famous [[:en:Forty-seven rōnin|Forty-seven rōnin]]. == Facilities == Two seated wooden male deity statues from the Heian period, said to represent {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}{{CulturalHeritageOnline|154691|木造男神坐像（伝天津日高日子穂穂出見命）}} and Shiotsuchi-no-Oji{{CulturalHeritageOnline|202150|木造男神坐像（伝塩椎神）}}, are designated as Important Cultural Properties of Japan. A large rock enshrined beside the main building, known as Ōiwa-sama, serves as a sacred object in Ōiwa Shrine. The shrine also has an inner shrine at the summit of Mt. Onigadake and a spring called "Ishigami no Yusui," which supplied the local water system until 1994. File:Oiwa shrine.jpg|Ōiwa Shrine File:Oiwa shrine object of worship (Oiwa-sama).jpg|Sacred object of Ōiwa Shrine (Ōiwa-sama) File:Ishigami's spring water.jpg|Ishigami no Yusui == Festivals == On August 15, the "Onigadake Fire Festival," in which participants ascend Mt. Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{translated page|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg |location = [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Echizen, Fukui|Echizen]], Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.900888888888886|N|136.12658333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})

The shrine’s priesthood has traditionally been held by the Okano family. Historical records confirm the family’s service from at least the Nanboku-chō period. Despite losing some members during Shibata Katsuie’s conflicts, the family lineage continues unbroken. The current chief priest, Okano Yoshinori, is considered the 100th in line. During the Edo period, some members of the family served as samurai in Akō Domain, with connections to the famous {{ill|Forty-seven rōnin|en|Forty-seven rōnin|WD=Q822472|lt=Forty-seven rōnin}}. == Facilities == Two seated wooden male deity statues from the Heian period, said to represent {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}{{CulturalHeritageOnline|154691|木造男神坐像（伝天津日高日子穂穂出見命）}} and Shiotsuchi-no-Oji{{CulturalHeritageOnline|202150|木造男神坐像（伝塩椎神）}}, are designated as Important Cultural Properties of Japan. A large rock enshrined beside the main building, known as Ōiwa-sama, serves as a sacred object in Ōiwa Shrine. The shrine also has an inner shrine at the summit of Mt. Onigadake and a spring called "Ishigami no Yusui," which supplied the local water system until 1994. File:Oiwa shrine.jpg|Ōiwa Shrine File:Oiwa shrine object of worship (Oiwa-sama).jpg|Sacred object of Ōiwa Shrine (Ōiwa-sama) File:Ishigami's spring water.jpg|Ishigami no Yusui == Festivals == On August 15, the "Onigadake Fire Festival," in which participants ascend Mt. Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]][[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg |location = {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.900888888888886|N|136.12658333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |consecration_year = 7th year of Emperor Sujin |MainHallStyle = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} |Festival = October 10 |Rituals = Onigadake Fire Festival |Map = {{location map|Japan Fukui Prefecture|label=Ōmushi Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} |location = {{ill|Echizen|WD=Q877941}} }} '''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōmushi Town, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) and a former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine is dedicated to the deity {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was established in the 7th year of Emperor Sujin’s reign, when the spirit of Hoori, who subdued and developed the Nan'etsu region, was enshrined at the summit of Mt. Onigadake. During Emperor Suinin's reign, a locust infestation was averted after prayers at the shrine, leading the emperor to name it Ōmushi Shrine and move it to its current location. The first historical mention appears in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}, which records that Ōmushi Shrine of Echizen Province's Nyū District was granted the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, in the ppth year of Hōki (780). By the 3rd year of Tentoku (959), it had risen to the highest Rank, Senior First Rank. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} as one of only two {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in Echizen Province, it held great importance. In 1576, the shrine buildings were burned down by Shibata Katsuie, but the sacred treasures were safely relocated to the summit of Mt. Onigadake. They were later restored under the order of Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1583, the shrine incorporated smaller shrines, including {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}, Amayo Shrine, and Raijin Shrine, although the latter two have contested identification. The shrine’s priesthood has traditionally been held by the Okano family. Historical records confirm the family’s service from at least the Nanboku-chō period. Despite losing some members during Shibata Katsuie’s conflicts, the family lineage continues unbroken. The current chief priest, Okano Yoshinori, is considered the 100th in line. During the Edo period, some members of the family served as samurai in Akō Domain, with connections to the famous {{ill|Forty-seven rōnin|en|Forty-seven rōnin|WD=Q822472|lt=Forty-seven rōnin}}. == Facilities == Two seated wooden male deity statues from the Heian period, said to represent {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}{{CulturalHeritageOnline|154691|木造男神坐像（伝天津日高日子穂穂出見命）}} and Shiotsuchi-no-Oji{{CulturalHeritageOnline|202150|木造男神坐像（伝塩椎神）}}, are designated as Important Cultural Properties of Japan. A large rock enshrined beside the main building, known as Ōiwa-sama, serves as a sacred object in Ōiwa Shrine. The shrine also has an inner shrine at the summit of Mt. Onigadake and a spring called "Ishigami no Yusui," which supplied the local water system until 1994. File:Oiwa shrine.jpg|Ōiwa Shrine File:Oiwa shrine object of worship (Oiwa-sama).jpg|Sacred object of Ōiwa Shrine (Ōiwa-sama) File:Ishigami's spring water.jpg|Ishigami no Yusui == Festivals == On August 15, the "Onigadake Fire Festival," in which participants ascend Mt. Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{translated page|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg |location = {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.900888888888886|N|136.12658333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})

Onigadake. During Emperor Suinin's reign, a locust infestation was averted after prayers at the shrine, leading the emperor to name it Ōmushi Shrine and move it to its current location. The first historical mention appears in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}, which records that Ōmushi Shrine of Echizen Province's Nyū District was granted the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, in the ppth year of Hōki (780). By the 3rd year of Tentoku (959), it had risen to the highest Rank, Senior First Rank. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} as one of only two {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in Echizen Province, it held great importance. In 1576, the shrine buildings were burned down by Shibata Katsuie, but the sacred treasures were safely relocated to the summit of Mt. Onigadake. They were later restored under the order of Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1583, the shrine incorporated smaller shrines, including {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}, Amayo Shrine, and Raijin Shrine, although the latter two have contested identification. The shrine’s priesthood has traditionally been held by the Okano family. Historical records confirm the family’s service from at least the Nanboku-chō period. Despite losing some members during Shibata Katsuie’s conflicts, the family lineage continues unbroken. The current chief priest, Okano Yoshinori, is considered the 100th in line. During the Edo period, some members of the family served as samurai in Akō Domain, with connections to the famous {{ill|Forty-seven rōnin|en|Forty-seven rōnin|WD=Q822472|lt=Forty-seven rōnin}}. == Facilities == Two seated wooden male deity statues from the Heian period, said to represent {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}{{CulturalHeritageOnline|154691|木造男神坐像（伝天津日高日子穂穂出見命）}} and Shiotsuchi-no-Oji{{CulturalHeritageOnline|202150|木造男神坐像（伝塩椎神）}}, are designated as Important Cultural Properties of Japan. A large rock enshrined beside the main building, known as Ōiwa-sama, serves as a sacred object in Ōiwa Shrine. The shrine also has an inner shrine at the summit of Mt. Onigadake and a spring called "Ishigami no Yusui," which supplied the local water system until 1994. File:Oiwa shrine.jpg|Ōiwa Shrine File:Oiwa shrine object of worship (Oiwa-sama).jpg|Sacred object of Ōiwa Shrine (Ōiwa-sama) File:Ishigami's spring water.jpg|Ishigami no Yusui == Festivals == On August 15, the "Onigadake Fire Festival," in which participants ascend Mt. Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]][[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg |location = {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.900888888888886|N|136.12658333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})

[[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg|300px|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen City)]] |location = {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|54|3.23|N|136|7|35.71|E|region:JP-18_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōmushi Shrine}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} |consecration_year = 7th year of Emperor Sujin |MainHallStyle = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} |Festival = October 10 |Rituals = Onigadake Fire Festival |Map = {{location map|Japan Fukui Prefecture|label=Ōmushi Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōmushi Town, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) and a former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. The shrine is dedicated to the deity {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was established in the 7th year of Emperor Sujin’s reign, when the spirit of Hoori, who subdued and developed the Nan'etsu region, was enshrined at the summit of Mt. Onigadake. During Emperor Suinin's reign, a locust infestation was averted after prayers at the shrine, leading the emperor to name it Ōmushi Shrine and move it to its current location. The first historical mention appears in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}, which records that Ōmushi Shrine of Echizen Province's Nyū District was granted the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, in the ppth year of Hōki (780). By the 3rd year of Tentoku (959), it had risen to the highest Rank, Senior First Rank. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} as one of only two {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in Echizen Province, it held great importance. In 1576, the shrine buildings were burned down by Shibata Katsuie, but the sacred treasures were safely relocated to the summit of Mt. Onigadake. They were later restored under the order of Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1583, the shrine incorporated smaller shrines, including {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}, Amayo Shrine, and Raijin Shrine, although the latter two have contested identification. The shrine’s priesthood has traditionally been held by the Okano family. Historical records confirm the family’s service from at least the Nanboku-chō period. Despite losing some members during Shibata Katsuie’s conflicts, the family lineage continues unbroken. The current chief priest, Okano Yoshinori, is considered the 100th in line. During the Edo period, some members of the family served as samurai in Akō Domain, with connections to the famous {{ill|Forty-seven rōnin|en|Forty-seven rōnin|WD=Q822472|lt=Forty-seven rōnin}}. == Facilities == Two seated wooden male deity statues from the Heian period, said to represent {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}{{CulturalHeritageOnline|154691|木造男神坐像（伝天津日高日子穂穂出見命）}} and Shiotsuchi-no-Oji{{CulturalHeritageOnline|202150|木造男神坐像（伝塩椎神）}}, are designated as Important Cultural Properties of Japan. A large rock enshrined beside the main building, known as Ōiwa-sama, serves as a sacred object in Ōiwa Shrine. The shrine also has an inner shrine at the summit of Mt. Onigadake and a spring called "Ishigami no Yusui," which supplied the local water system until 1994. File:Oiwa shrine.jpg|Ōiwa Shrine File:Oiwa shrine object of worship (Oiwa-sama).jpg|Sacred object of Ōiwa Shrine (Ōiwa-sama) File:Ishigami's spring water.jpg|Ishigami no Yusui == Festivals == On August 15, the "Onigadake Fire Festival," in which participants ascend Mt. Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]][[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg |location = {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.900888888888886|N|136.12658333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})

Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{translated page|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} [[Category:Buildings in Echizen city]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Former_Prefectural_Shrines_in_Fukui_Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Echizen city]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Omushi errors]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:pages with translation template added]] [[Category:pages without valid translation templates or en interwikis]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Fukui prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echizen Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg|300px|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen City)]] |location = {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|54|3.23|N|136|7|35.71|E|region:JP-18_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōmushi Shrine}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} |consecration_year = 7th year of Emperor Sujin |MainHallStyle = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} |Festival = October 10 |Rituals = Onigadake Fire Festival |Map = {{location map|Japan Fukui Prefecture|label=Ōmushi Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōmushi Town, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) and a former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. The shrine is dedicated to the deity {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was established in the 7th year of Emperor Sujin’s reign, when the spirit of Hoori, who subdued and developed the Nan'etsu region, was enshrined at the summit of Mt. Onigadake. During Emperor Suinin's reign, a locust infestation was averted after prayers at the shrine, leading the emperor to name it Ōmushi Shrine and move it to its current location. The first historical mention appears in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}, which records that Ōmushi Shrine of Echizen Province's Nyū District was granted the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, in the ppth year of Hōki (780). By the 3rd year of Tentoku (959), it had risen to the highest Rank, Senior First Rank. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} as one of only two {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in Echizen Province, it held great importance. In 1576, the shrine buildings were burned down by Shibata Katsuie, but the sacred treasures were safely relocated to the summit of Mt. Onigadake. They were later restored under the order of Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1583, the shrine incorporated smaller shrines, including {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}, Amayo Shrine, and Raijin Shrine, although the latter two have contested identification. The shrine’s priesthood has traditionally been held by the Okano family. Historical records confirm the family’s service from at least the Nanboku-chō period. Despite losing some members during Shibata Katsuie’s conflicts, the family lineage continues unbroken. The current chief priest, Okano Yoshinori, is considered the 100th in line. During the Edo period, some members of the family served as samurai in Akō Domain, with connections to the famous {{ill|Forty-seven rōnin|en|Forty-seven rōnin|WD=Q822472|lt=Forty-seven rōnin}}. == Facilities == Two seated wooden male deity statues from the Heian period, said to represent {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}{{CulturalHeritageOnline|154691|木造男神坐像（伝天津日高日子穂穂出見命）}} and Shiotsuchi-no-Oji{{CulturalHeritageOnline|202150|木造男神坐像（伝塩椎神）}}, are designated as Important Cultural Properties of Japan. A large rock enshrined beside the main building, known as Ōiwa-sama, serves as a sacred object in Ōiwa Shrine. The shrine also has an inner shrine at the summit of Mt. Onigadake and a spring called "Ishigami no Yusui," which supplied the local water system until 1994. File:Oiwa shrine.jpg|Ōiwa Shrine File:Oiwa shrine object of worship (Oiwa-sama).jpg|Sacred object of Ōiwa Shrine (Ōiwa-sama) File:Ishigami's spring water.jpg|Ishigami no Yusui == Festivals == On August 15, the "Onigadake Fire Festival," in which participants ascend Mt. Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]][[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11438783]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg |location = {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.900888888888886|N|136.12658333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |consecration_year = 7th year of Emperor Sujin |MainHallStyle = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} |Festival = October 10 |Rituals = Onigadake Fire Festival |Map = {{location map|Japan Fukui Prefecture|label=Ōmushi Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} |location = {{ill|Echizen|WD=Q877941}} }} '''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōmushi Town, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) and a former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine’s priesthood has traditionally been held by the Okano family. Historical records confirm the family’s service from at least the Nanboku-chō period. Despite losing some members during Shibata Katsuie’s conflicts, the family lineage continues unbroken. The current chief priest, Okano Yoshinori, is considered the 100th in line. During the Edo period, some members of the family served as samurai in Akō Domain, with connections to the famous {{ill|Forty-seven rōnin|en|Forty-seven rōnin|WD=Q822472|lt=Forty-seven rōnin}}. == Facilities == Two seated wooden male deity statues from the Heian period, said to represent {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}{{CulturalHeritageOnline|154691|木造男神坐像（伝天津日高日子穂穂出見命）}} and Shiotsuchi-no-Oji{{CulturalHeritageOnline|202150|木造男神坐像（伝塩椎神）}}, are designated as Important Cultural Properties of Japan. A large rock enshrined beside the main building, known as Ōiwa-sama, serves as a sacred object in Ōiwa Shrine. The shrine also has an inner shrine at the summit of Mt. Onigadake and a spring called "Ishigami no Yusui," which supplied the local water system until 1994. File:Oiwa shrine.jpg|Ōiwa Shrine File:Oiwa shrine object of worship (Oiwa-sama).jpg|Sacred object of Ōiwa Shrine (Ōiwa-sama) File:Ishigami's spring water.jpg|Ishigami no Yusui == Festivals == On August 15, the "Onigadake Fire Festival," in which participants ascend Mt. Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{translated page|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} [[Category:Buildings in Echizen city]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Former_Prefectural_Shrines_in_Fukui_Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Echizen city]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Omushi errors]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:pages with translation template added]] [[Category:pages without valid translation templates or en interwikis]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Fukui prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echizen Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmushi Shrine (大虫神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oomushi shrine(Echizen city).jpg|300px|Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen City)]] |location = {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, Ōmushi Town 21-28 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|54|3.23|N|136|7|35.71|E|region:JP-18_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōmushi Shrine}} |deity = {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}} |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} |consecration_year = 7th year of Emperor Sujin |MainHallStyle = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} |Festival = October 10 |Rituals = Onigadake Fire Festival |Map = {{location map|Japan Fukui Prefecture|label=Ōmushi Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Ōmushi Shrine''' (大虫神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōmushi Town, {{ill|Echizen, Fukui|en|Echizen, Fukui|WD=Q877941|lt=Echizen}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) and a former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. The shrine is dedicated to the deity {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was established in the 7th year of Emperor Sujin’s reign, when the spirit of Hoori, who subdued and developed the Nan'etsu region, was enshrined at the summit of Mt. Onigadake. During Emperor Suinin's reign, a locust infestation was averted after prayers at the shrine, leading the emperor to name it Ōmushi Shrine and move it to its current location. The first historical mention appears in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}, which records that Ōmushi Shrine of Echizen Province's Nyū District was granted the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, in the ppth year of Hōki (780). By the 3rd year of Tentoku (959), it had risen to the highest Rank, Senior First Rank. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} as one of only two {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in Echizen Province, it held great importance. In 1576, the shrine buildings were burned down by Shibata Katsuie, but the sacred treasures were safely relocated to the summit of Mt. Onigadake. They were later restored under the order of Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1583, the shrine incorporated smaller shrines, including {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}, Amayo Shrine, and Raijin Shrine, although the latter two have contested identification. The shrine’s priesthood has traditionally been held by the Okano family. Historical records confirm the family’s service from at least the Nanboku-chō period. Despite losing some members during Shibata Katsuie’s conflicts, the family lineage continues unbroken. The current chief priest, Okano Yoshinori, is considered the 100th in line. During the Edo period, some members of the family served as samurai in Akō Domain, with connections to the famous {{ill|Forty-seven rōnin|en|Forty-seven rōnin|WD=Q822472|lt=Forty-seven rōnin}}. == Facilities == Two seated wooden male deity statues from the Heian period, said to represent {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}{{CulturalHeritageOnline|154691|木造男神坐像（伝天津日高日子穂穂出見命）}} and Shiotsuchi-no-Oji{{CulturalHeritageOnline|202150|木造男神坐像（伝塩椎神）}}, are designated as Important Cultural Properties of Japan. A large rock enshrined beside the main building, known as Ōiwa-sama, serves as a sacred object in Ōiwa Shrine. The shrine also has an inner shrine at the summit of Mt. Onigadake and a spring called "Ishigami no Yusui," which supplied the local water system until 1994. Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) and a former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. The first historical mention appears in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}, which records that Ōmushi Shrine of Echizen Province's Nyū District was granted the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, in the ppth year of Hōki (780). By the 3rd year of Tentoku (959), it had risen to the highest Rank, Senior First Rank. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} as one of only two {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in Echizen Province, it held great importance. In 1576, the shrine buildings were burned down by Shibata Katsuie, but the sacred treasures were safely relocated to the summit of Mt. Onigadake. They were later restored under the order of Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1583, the shrine incorporated smaller shrines, including {{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}, Amayo Shrine, and Raijin Shrine, although the latter two have contested identification. The shrine’s priesthood has traditionally been held by the Okano family. Historical records confirm the family’s service from at least the Nanboku-chō period. Despite losing some members during Shibata Katsuie’s conflicts, the family lineage continues unbroken. The current chief priest, Okano Yoshinori, is considered the 100th in line. During the Edo period, some members of the family served as samurai in Akō Domain, with connections to the famous {{ill|Forty-seven rōnin|en|Forty-seven rōnin|WD=Q822472|lt=Forty-seven rōnin}}. == Facilities == Two seated wooden male deity statues from the Heian period, said to represent {{ill|Hoori|ja|天津日高彦火火出見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホオリ}}{{CulturalHeritageOnline|154691|木造男神坐像（伝天津日高日子穂穂出見命）}} and Shiotsuchi-no-Oji{{CulturalHeritageOnline|202150|木造男神坐像（伝塩椎神）}}, are designated as Important Cultural Properties of Japan. A large rock enshrined beside the main building, known as Ōiwa-sama, serves as a sacred object in Ōiwa Shrine. The shrine also has an inner shrine at the summit of Mt. Onigadake and a spring called "Ishigami no Yusui," which supplied the local water system until 1994. File:Oiwa shrine.jpg|Ōiwa Shrine File:Oiwa shrine object of worship (Oiwa-sama).jpg|Sacred object of Ōiwa Shrine (Ōiwa-sama) File:Ishigami's spring water.jpg|Ishigami no Yusui == Festivals == On August 15, the "Onigadake Fire Festival," in which participants ascend Mt. Onigadake with torches, is held. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Seated wooden male deity statue (Hoori){{Cite web|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/choukoku/omushijinja-danshin.html|title=Fukui Prefecture Cultural Properties: Wooden Seated Male deity Statues (Hoori and Shiotsuchi)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|accessdate=2015-pp-23}} * Seated wooden male deity statue (Shiotsuchi-no-Oji) == Miyabashi == The stone single-arch bridge known as "Miyabashi" spans the Ōmushi River within the shrine grounds. Built using locally quarried stone resistant to freezing and snow damage, the bridge measures 7.9 meters in length and 3.1 meters in width. It was completed in 1919 after seven years of construction and registered as a Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in 2001.{{CulturalHeritageOnline|148995|大虫神社宮橋}} File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than downstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (downstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (than upstream side).jpg|Miyabashi (upstream view) File:Miyahashi in Omushi shrine (road side).jpg|Miyabashi (road view) == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社 (越前市)]] }} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[ja:大虫神社]] [[ja:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{translated page|ja|大虫神社|version=39256620|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[sn:Ōmushi Shrine (Echizen)]] {{wikidata link|Q11438783}} [[Category:Buildings in Echizen city]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Former_Prefectural_Shrines_in_Fukui_Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Echizen city]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Omushi errors]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:pages with translation template added]] [[Category:pages without valid translation templates or en interwikis]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Fukui prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echizen Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]]
